summary:
Zoroastrianism in the Achaemenid Empire (c. 500–330 BC) was the dominant religion of the Persian ruling class, centered on the teachings of the prophet Zoroaster (Zarathustra) — a dualistic faith pitting the supreme good deity Ahura Mazda against the evil spirit Angra Mainyu — whose influence on Western monotheism through its concepts of heaven, hell, judgment, and cosmic dualism was profound and enduring.

The Achaemenid kings, from Cyrus the Great onward, identified themselves as servants of Ahura Mazda, and royal inscriptions routinely begin with this deity's name. However, Persian religious policy was notably tolerant: [[event:1097|Chandragupta Maurya]]'s predecessor Cyrus restored Jewish exiles to Palestine and respected local religious traditions across the empire, while [[event:1139|Darius the Great Reforms]]' administration accommodated temples and priests of multiple traditions within the satrapal system. Zoroastrianism's emphasis on truth, righteousness, and cosmic dualism introduced concepts that influenced subsequent Jewish theology during the Babylonian exile, and through Judaism influenced Christianity and Islam — including notions of a final judgment, resurrection of the dead, Satan as adversary, and a messianic savior. The religion survived the empire's fall and remains a living tradition today (as Zoroastrianism/Parsism).
